coredata.dc:title: Lysosome-targeted carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode detection of iron ion, in vitro and in vivo imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Dan
Zhao, Zhonghua
Shi, Lihong
Liu, Wenliang
Yang, Yongxing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode sensor based on lysosome-targeted CDs has been desirably implemented to identify Fe3+ fluctuations in vitro and in vivo. By simple one-pot hydrothermal carbonization of dried field mint, yellow-fluorescent CDs were directly fabricated without the assistance of other reagents and hold exceptional stability, superior biocompatibility as well as ultra-low cytotoxicity. Results indicated that as-prepared CDs can provide a rapid, reliable, and highly selective recognition of Fe3+ with a linear range of 0 μM–400 μM and a detection limit of 0.037 μM. Impressively, it was found that as-developed CDs can successfully target lysosome with high colocalization coefficient (0.85) and responds to fluctuations of Fe3+ in living cells. Further, acquired CDs was ingeniously devoted to Escherichia coli imaging. Besides, obtained CDs was eventually utilized to track the variation of Fe3+ in vivo system. A preliminary research expresses that as-synthesized CDs can function as an effective tool to detect Fe3+ in vitro and in vivo and thus indicates the promising applicability for disease detection in physiology and pathology in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Yellow-fluorescent
Carbon dots
Iron ion
Target
Imaging
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent and colorimetric detection of Fe3+
In vitro imaging and targeted lysosome for CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874087 ) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talent Project .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs and application in Fe3+ sensing and bioimaging.
(A) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra of CDs. (B) PL spectra of CDs at wide range of excitation wavelengths (400–490 nm). (C) CIE chromaticity coordinates of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence responses of CDs over various ions (400 μM). (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs upon various amounts of Fe3+. (C) Linear relationship of Ln(F/F0-0.25) and Fe3+ concentrations. (D) Color changes of CDs solution after adding various ions under sunlight (Ln+ = 400 μM). (E) Color changes of CDs solution at different amounts of Fe3+ under sunlight. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
CDs and Lyso Tracker Green colocalization imaging in HeLa cells. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Images in HeLa cells of CDs with Fe3+ in 40 s.
Images in E. coli of CDs.
Images in zebrafish larvae of CDs and Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Lysosome-targeted carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode detection of iron ion, in vitro and in vivo imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode sensor based on lysosome-targeted CDs has been desirably implemented to identify Fe3+ fluctuations in vitro and in vivo. By simple one-pot hydrothermal carbonization of dried field mint, yellow-fluorescent CDs were directly fabricated without the assistance of other reagents and hold exceptional stability, superior biocompatibility as well as ultra-low cytotoxicity. Results indicated that as-prepared CDs can provide a rapid, reliable, and highly selective recognition of Fe3+ with a linear range of 0 μM–400 μM and a detection limit of 0.037 μM. Impressively, it was found that as-developed CDs can successfully target lysosome with high colocalization coefficient (0.85) and responds to fluctuations of Fe3+ in living cells. Further, acquired CDs was ingeniously devoted to Escherichia coli imaging. Besides, obtained CDs was eventually utilized to track the variation of Fe3+ in vivo system. A preliminary research expresses that as-synthesized CDs can function as an effective tool to detect Fe3+ in vitro and in vivo and thus indicates the promising applicability for disease detection in physiology and pathology in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging star of carbon nanomaterials, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have gained dramatic concern on account of captivating superiorities in terms of adjustable photoluminescence, exceptional photostability, ultra-low toxicity as well as prominent biocompatibility. Until now, fluorescent CDs have been found more promising applications in an extensive range of fields including catalysis [1–3], fingerprint detection [4–6], bioimaging [7–9], optoelectronic devices [10–12], and so forth [13–15]. Especially, CDs have been recognized as an excellent candidate comparing with traditional fluorescent materials for bioimaging involving diverse live cell imaging as well as bacteria imaging. Song et al. [16] built a multifunctional ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dual-emissive CDs to visualize lysine and pH fluctuations in HeLa cells and Escherichia coli. Jiao et al. [17] developed nitrogen-doped CDs-based sensing platform by the fluorescence “turn-off-on” mode for tracking silver ions and glutathione in aqueous solution as well as in SMMC 7721 cells. Dong et al. [18] ingeniously designed color-tunable luminescence CDs through adjusting the pH of precursor solutions for application in multicolored intracellular imaging. However, most of reported probes focused on their applications for imaging research in vitro, which seriously hindered deeper insight into the physiological and pathological roles of various harmful substances. On this basis, construction of CDs-based fluorescent sensors suitable for monitoring chemical analytes in vivo is fundamentally significant and valuable.

Iron (Fe), as one of the utmost components of human body, participates in diverse pathological and physiological processes such as nucleic acid synthesis, electronic transfer, metabolism of various enzymes, oxygen transportation and so forth [19]. Most worryingly, inadequate or excessive intake of Fe3+ from the normal permissible limit will induce imbalance of cell homeostasis and trigger numerous biological disorders, which are associated with undesirable diseases in term of anemia, heart attack, intellectual deterioration and cancer [20]. Inspired by over reasons, it is extremely necessary and urgently needed to achieve reliable and precise assays for analysis of Fe3+ contents. To date, multiple analytical methods have been frequently developed, such as spectrophotometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and so forth [21–23]. Among these strategies, the accurate and rapid determination of Fe3+ is limited because of several inevitable influence factors, including sophisticated instrumentation, intricate sample synthesis steps and tedious operation. In contrast, CDs-based florescent Fe3+ probes are preferred to avoid these drawbacks and have been regarded as a hallmark sensing system to track Fe3+ benefiting from their unique features including rapidity, simplicity and cheapness. Lu et al. [24] employed dl-malic acid, ethanolamine and ethane-sulfonic acid to synthesize N, S co-doped CDs via microwave-assisted pyrolysis which has been successfully extended for timely monitoring Fe3+ fluctuations in vitro. Han et al. [25] reported highly blue luminescent N-doped CDs by simply pyrolytic strategy using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid and urea for label-free determination of Fe3+. Huang et al. [26] constructed a novel fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ which were obtained through the conjugate connection of CDs and desferrioxamine B. Also, the colorimetric and fluorescent bimodal strategy has sparked a great deal of concern and been confirmed to be a profoundly efficient tool in biomedical fields because these bimodal probes not only offer ultra-sensitive fluorescence method but also contribute to the visualization of analytes by naked eyes. Shi et al. [20] developed yellow-fluorescent CDs for colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode determination of Fe3+ which can be readily visualized via remarkable color changes under sunlight. Liu et al. [27] synthesized CDs through hydrothermal route of 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid and 4-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride which have been further used for the selective fluorometric and colorimetric analysis of Fe3+. Liu et al. [28] ingeniously constructed bright blue-fluorescent CDs with melamine by a neutralization heat reaction which were implemented to quantitatively detect Fe3+ for fluorometric and colorimetric strategy. Currently, the exploitation of CDs-based fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode Fe3+ sensors are still at an early-stage. Therefore, it is very valuable to establish novel colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode probes for widespread application.

Inspired by above-mentioned issues, novel CDs was fabricated via hydrothermal route using dried field mint. Acquired CDs are better proven to be not only ultra-low toxicity, but also to perform prominent biocompatibility and strong stability. Charmingly, a colorimetric and fluorescent bimodal sensor based on CDs was constructed to monitor Fe3+ in aqueous solution. Meanwhile, as-synthesized CDs have also excellent targeting effect towards lysosome and been further extended to visualize Fe3+ fluctuations in vitro and in vivo.

0.5 g solid powder were first dispersed in ultrapure water (20 mL), and the solution was sealed in a Teflon autoclave followed by keeping at 200 °C for 5 h. Afterwards, the crude products were centrifuged under 3000 rpm for 0.5 h. Next, the supernatant was ﬁltered via a microporous membrane (0.22 mm). Ultimately, CDs were acquired as a solid-state CDs through freeze-drying.

Assay of the detection of Fe3+ were conducted at room temperature. At first, the titration experiment of CDs towards Fe3+ was performed in Tris-HCl buffer solution (7.2). Specifically, 100 μL CDs aqueous solution was dispersed in 500 μL Tris-HCl buffer solution. After this, various amounts of Fe3+ were introduced to CDs system for 30 s. Upon the identical detection condition, cations as well as anions selectivity studies were performed instead of Fe3+. For all the studies, the fluorescence was recorded at the excitation of 471 nm.

The CDs were acquired by hydrothermal carbonization of dried field mint, as presented in Fig. 1 . In contrast to traditional preparation, no more additional modification or any further conjugation was required. This route is easy to synthesize, facile in operation and environmentally friendly. Normally, original material is readily available, cheap and green carbon raw containing many elements, which contributes to the requirement of the fabrication of CDs. We utilized dried field mint as original material, which has ingredients of carbohydrates and macromolecule compounds such as alcohols, flavonoids, amino acids, organic acids and terpenoids. Briefly, these compounds were originally hydrolyzed, dehydrated and decomposed to obtain soluble compounds, which were further transformed into diverse polymeric intermediates after reactions of polymerization and condensation. Ultimately, aromatization, carbonization and nuclear burst gave rise to the formation of CDs [29–31].

A close observation shows that as-prepared CDs are monodispersed and uniformly dispersed without apparent aggregation in Fig. S1A. From the size distribution view of TEM image in Fig. S1B, it is seen that CDs is mainly concentrated in range of 5.5 nm–8.5 nm with the average diameter of 6.66 ± 0.83 nm. FTIR analysis of CDs is presented in Fig. S2. The characteristic band of CDs appears at around 3190 cm−1 and 2942 cm−1 which match with the stretching vibration of O–H and C–H, respectively. The surface of CDs contains CO (1595 cm−1) functional group. Two absorption peaks at around 1331 cm−1 and 1274 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending vibration of C–H and O–H functional group, respectively. A peak at about 1111 cm−1 confirms the presence of C–O. The element composition and valence state of obtained CDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) as shown in Fig. S2. The full range XPS (Fig. S2B) shows that CDs contain carbon, nitrogen and oxygen and the ratio of them is 2.55: 1: 3.23. The high-resolution C1s spectra peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.6 eV and 287.8 eV in Fig. S2C confirm the formation of CC, C–O and CO bond, respectively. The N1s band in Fig. S2D shows three peaks at 399.7 eV for C–N–C, 400.6 eV for N–(C3) and 401.8 eV for N–H. In the O1s spectrum from Fig. S2E, a peak at around 531.5 eV matches with CO bond, whereas the peak at 532.8 eV is in good agreement with C–O.

Fig. 2 A presented that CDs have a distinct absorption peak at 277 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, which is due to the absorption of π−π* transition of the conjugated CC [32–42]. By changing the excitation wavelengths altering from 400 to 490 nm, it is clear in Fig. 2B that the PL intensity of as-acquired CDs first increases from 400 to 470 nm, and then decreases at the range of 470–490 nm, which presents the feature of red shift and probably corresponds to the influence of the size-dependent luminescence or the distribution of emissive traps on CDs [43]. Further, the optimal excitation of 471 corresponds to the maximum emission wavelength of 531 nm. It is clearly described in Fig. 2C that fluorescence emissions of CDs were better calculated with chromaticity diagram (0.28, 0.65). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs reaches 6.9%. Moreover, the fluorescence of as-acquired CDs keeps considerably stable in a wide range of pH (3.0–9.0) as presented in Fig. S3, which indicated that the fluorescence of obtained CDs is pH independent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent and colorimetric detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of assessing the fluorescence responses towards multiple analytes, the selective experiments of CDs were further implemented. As can be seen from Fig. 3 A, other competitive analytes induced no tremendous variations, except that Fe3+ displays the largest quenching of the fluorescence of CDs in aqueous solution. Apart from cations, there are no distinct influences of anions for the fluorescence property of CDs after adding multiple anions in Fig. S4. Similarly, the large amounts of other analytes have almost no any signal interferences, which implied that acquired CDs can be effectively served as a “turn−off” sensor for tracking Fe3+. Upon the introduction of Fe3+ altering 0 μM from 400 μM, the FL intensity reduced progressively in Fig. 3B. From Fig. 3C, a friendly linear relationship between Ln(F/F0-0.25) and Fe3+ was clearly presented in the scope of 0 μM–400 μM (R2 = 0.995), where F and F0 were the FL intensity of CDs with and without the presence of Fe3+, respectively. Notably, the detection limit was 0.037 μM. In comparison with those CDs derived from various other natural resources in Table S1, our proposed CDs for detecting Fe3+ presented a lower value which indicated that this sensor is more sensitive. In addition, this sensor also has wider detection range to extend its further application under larger range of Fe3+ concentrations condition. Moreover, colorimetric sensing behavior of CDs towards Fe3+ was further executed. As expected, it is observed by naked eyes in Fig. 3D that the color of CDs solution apparently changed from pale yellow to dark-brown after the introduction of 400 μM Fe3+, while other common cations cause no variation or slight changes in color, which is probably ascribed to complex formation interaction between CDs and Fe3+. Besides, it is extremely charming that Fe3+ can be visualized with this sensor through obvious color variations of the solution. In Fig. 3E, the solution color of CDs varied from pale yellow to dark-brown in the scope of 0 μM–400 μM, which suggested that this sensor can be applied to identify Fe3+ succinctly and intuitively under sunlight.

In view of the above results, we naturally made a thorough survey to explain the optical response of CDs towards Fe3+ employing UV/vis absorption spectra of CDs and CDs/Fe3+. More details indicated in Fig. S5A that there was a notable enhancement in the absorption spectrum of CDs after the continuous addition of Fe3+, which is due to the coordination of CDs and Fe3+ [20,31]. Based on this phenomenon, we deduced that the dynamic quenching behavior could be negligible in this case and the interaction between Fe3+ and CDs is possibly in line with the formation of a stable non-fluorescence complex. More important evidence confirmed that upon introducing Fe3+ into CDs system, the fluorescence lifetime exhibited slight variation from 6.58 ns to 6.52 ns (Fig. S5B). It can be inferred that CDs undergoes a static quenching mechanism and Fe3+ indeed reacted with CDs to generate the new substance [33]. All the above consequences exhibited that the interaction mechanism for CDs towards Fe3+ can stem from static quenching which probably form a stable complex between CDs and Fe3+. As we all know, as-acquired CDs prefers to bind with iron (III) ions over other metal ions, which may arise from the special coordination/chelation interactions of Fe3+ and the N and O containing functional groups of as-prepared CDs. Specifically, Fe3+ as transition metal hold unfilled d orbital to accept the electrons. After introducing Fe3+, the N and O containing groups of acquired-CDs reacted rapidly with Fe3+ to result in the non-radiative electron transfer that involved partial transfer of an electron in the excited state into the d orbital of Fe3+, which caused the distinct fluorescence quenching of CDs [44–50].

Notably, the experiment for iron detection were carried out at pH 7.2. Under such conditions, as-acquired CDs almost completely reacted with Fe3+, and the remaining Fe3+ concentration in the solution is extremely low. The ion product of Fe(OH)3 (Qi) is far less than the Ksp of Fe (OH)3 at 25 °C, and thus there is no the precipitation of Fe(OH)3. In addition, considering the complicated media environments of actual life, analysis of Fe3+ under neutral and alkaline conditions were further conducted to evaluate whether as-prepared CDs can still identify Fe3+. Fig. S6 indicated that as-acquired CDs can still generate drastic response towards Fe3+ even in the neutral or alkaline state of pH, and there is no any precipitation. This consequence strongly confirmed that the fluorescence quenching of obtained CDs under neutral or alkaline conditions corresponded to the strong coordination rather than the precipitation of Fe(OH)3.

Moreover, the practicability analysis of Fe3+ in tap water sample was further conducted and the consequence was listed in Table S2. It is found that the recoveries of Fe3+ in tap water concentrated in 95.24%–102.52%, which strongly validated that as-acquired CDs can act as an ideal fluorescent probe for detection of Fe3+ in the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro imaging and targeted lysosome for CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by Fe3+ sensing in watery solution together with superior biocompatibility, it is highly desirable to explore the applicability of this novel fluorescence sensor in biological fields. Prior to intracellular bioimaging and biosensing of Fe3+, the cytotoxicity of CDs was assessed by MTT assay with HeLa cells. As presented in Fig. S7, no marked drop of cell viability was visualized towards cells stained with CDs at a high concentration (up to 0.8 mg/mL). Besides, when as-synthesized CDs was introduced into the growth medium to incubate for 24 h, HeLa cells were fusiform in shape, and no changes in cell morphology were found even at higher concentrations, proving that this probe has ultra-low toxicity in vitro. Typically, the florescent probes are considered to hold an excellent lysosome targeting effect when the colocalization coefficient is reach to or more than 0.5 (τ ≥ 0.5) [51]. Accordingly, we deeply evaluated the distribution of CDs in HeLa cells and a colocalization experiment with Lyso Tracker Green was performed in Fig. 4 . It was clearly observed that as-prepared CDs and Lyso Tracker Green overlapped well in two channels and the colocalization coefficient is 0.85 for lysosome, which indicated that obtained CDs can permeate into organelles and have good targeting effect on lysosome.

Followed by that, HeLa cells were cultivated through as-prepared CDs for 0.5 h and tracked through confocal microscopy under excitation of 405 nm, 488 nm, and 514 nm lasers. As expected, a notable fluorescence emitted from the intracellular region as shown in Fig. 5 , which suggested CDs have the favorable membrane permeability and transferred into living cells. Results showed that when Fe3+ were added in the growth medium under 40 s, images of HeLa cells expressed weak intracellular fluorescence, which further testified that this probe could be applied for analysis of intracellular Fe3+ fluctuations in real time.

In addition, Escherichia coli (E. coli) were frequently used as the model system for farther application of as-synthesized CDs on bacteria imaging. Accordingly, cytotoxicity determination was preliminarily conducted. From Fig. S8, it indicated that no evident reduction of E. coli viability was seen when concentration of CDs reached up to 40 μg/mL, confirming the ultra-low influences of obtained CDs to E. coli. More significantly, it was observed that E. coli presented a clear short rod shape and there was no visible inhibition of E. coli growth when various contents of obtained CDs were added in culture medium. Take advantage of these merits, bacteria were tagged with CDs to probe the potential of biolabeling properties of CDs. From Fig. 6 E.coli stained with CDs indicated bright fluorescent in three channels (blue, yellow, and red), which distinctly showed that CDs have no bactericidal properties and this consequence further broadens the applications of CDs in microbial imaging.

More significantly, we investigate the practicality of developed probe in zebrafish larvae to widen their applications. Similarly, a series of samples with changing different amounts of CDs were performed and initially analyzed the long-term viability of zebrafish larvae. It was found in Fig. S9 that zebrafish larvae exposed to CDs even up to 0.5 mg/mL presented a slight variation in numbers, which indicated that as-acquired CDs have no influences for zebrafish larvae growth. From Fig. 7 , it indicated that zebrafish larvae labeled with CDs displayed distinct fluorescent signal in both yellow and red channels, including that obtained CDs penetrate smoothly the entire body of the zebrafish and thus can be capable of imaging in vivo. Meaningfully, after the introduction of Fe3+ in culture dishes, an obvious reduction in fluorescence emission was captured, which showed that as-acquired CDs can be implemented successfully as a visual sensing tool for monitoring Fe3+ variations. Our proposed CDs have achieved in vivo imaging and there are very few reports of CDs derived from natural biomass for monitoring Fe3+ in vivo with satisfying results to the best of our knowledge.

In summary, yellow-fluorescent CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization of dried field mint, which hold exceptional biocompatibility, ultra-low cytotoxicity and selectivity toward Fe3+. Benefiting from these inherent merits of CDs, it has been further implemented in measurement of Fe3+ with a colorimetric and fluorescent bimodal method. Furthermore, developed CDs can excellently target lysosome. In addition, this designed sensor been utilized to visualize Fe3+ variations in vivo and in vitro with satisfying results. We believe that our present research can be applied as a novel promising candidate for Fe3+ sensing in vivo and in vitro, as well as their practical applications in wide ranging fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talent Project.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122423.

Dan Chang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing-original draft, Zhonghua Zhao: Investigation, Validation, Lihong Shi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing-review & editing, Wenliang Liu: Investigation, Data curation, Yongxing Yang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing-review & editing.
